Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

:

Student_2
Student_3
Student_4
Student_5

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

:

Student_3
Student_4
Student_5

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.
The player characters are:

content_store.fe4e83f362910a21:
Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

The player characters are:
:
Student_1
Student_2
Student_3
Student_4
Student_5

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

content_store.b706f4b4a04fb00a:
Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

Student_3
Student_4
Student_5

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.
The player characters are:
:
Student_1
Student_2
Student_3
Student_4
Student_5

Background info:
[observation] [putative_event] Student_3 nodded in agreement with Student_1's proposal, appreciating the emphasis on independent work. "I like that approach, Student_1. Working on separate modules with clear APIs means we can each really focus on our piece without constantly waiting for others. It definitely minimizes coordination overhead, which I think will help us get things done efficiently, especially with the demo only three weeks away."  He paused, thinking for a moment. "I could take on developing the core backend service that handles user authentication and profile management. It's a critical component, and it fits well into that 'backend layer' you mentioned. I can start looking into a good framework for that, maybe something like Flask or Node.js with Express, and get a basic API prototype going by the end of the week."

I could look into setting up the basic user interface for the login and registration pages this week, getting those components ready to connect to Student_3's authentication service once it's up." 0).  It is week 7 of a 10-week undergraduate computer science group project. Five students have been assigned to build a working software system together for their final project. The team must decide on architecture, divide coding tasks, coordinate through GitHub, respond to bugs, and prepare a final demo. Team members differ in personality, motivation, technical ability, and comfort with collaboration. Social loafing, uneven task distribution, leadership struggles, and deadline stress may emerge over time.  The team is meeting after class to decide how to organize the project. After Student_4 remained silent for a moment, Student_2 spoke, looking directly at Student_1, "I agree with the modular architecture, Student_1. It definitely seems like the most efficient way for us to make progress quickly and independently, which is crucial with the demo approaching." Student_2 then shifted their gaze to the whiteboard, tapping a finger on their chin thoughtfully. "Since Student_3 is tackling the core backend services and Student_5 the initial UI, I can take responsibility for the data layer. This includes designing the database schema, setting up the database itself – I'd lean towards PostgreSQL for its robustness – and implementing a robust data access layer. I can get the initial schema designed and the basic DAL implemented by the end of this week, ensuring it's ready for Student_3's authentication service to interact with." Student_2 paused, then added, "Also, I'd be happy to set up our GitHub repository with a clear branching strategy and some basic pull request guidelines to keep things organized as we work independently."

Student_4 observed that the primary architectural components were assigned. Student_4 then spoke, directing their gaze at the group, "It sounds like the core services are well-covered. 2.  **Student_4** remained silent, observing the discussion.
3.  **Student_3** agreed with the modular approach, citing its efficiency and reduced coordination overhead, especially with the deadline looming. He volunteered to develop the core backend service for user authentication and profile management, aiming for a prototype by week's end using Flask or Node.js.
4.  **Student_5** also supported the modular approach for independent work and quick progress. She offered to set up the basic front-end user interface for login and registration pages, ready to connect to Student_3's authentication service. She then looked at Student_4.
5.  **Student_2**, noticing Student_4's continued silence, agreed with the modular architecture's efficiency. He volunteered to handle the data layer, including designing the PostgreSQL database schema and implementing a robust data access layer, also by week's end. Additionally, he offered to set up the GitHub repository with a branching strategy and pull request guidelines.
6.  **Student_4**, seeing that the core architectural components were assigned, offered to build secondary UI components (like user profile display), set up basic testing frameworks, or write initial API documentation. They expressed willingness to take on any supporting role that wouldn't overlap with existing assignments.

content_store.7d8d4735808c75e6:
Action: Student_1 nodded, a sense of satisfaction in their expression as the team's responsibilities solidified. They looked at the group, particularly at Student_2 and Student_3 who had embraced the modular approach, and then at Student_5 and Student_4 who had found clear, independent contributions.

"Excellent," Student_1 stated, their voice clear and confident. "It sounds like we have a very solid plan in place, and everyone has a clear piece of the puzzle to focus on. To recap, Student_3 will handle the core backend services, particularly authentication and user profile management. Student_5 will tackle the initial UI for login and registration, getting those components ready to integrate. Student_2 will take on the data layer, including schema design, database setup with PostgreSQL, and the data access layer, as well as setting up our GitHub repository with a clear branching strategy. And Student_4, your offer to build out secondary UI components, user profile displays, and critically, establish testing frameworks and API documentation, will be invaluable for quality and clarity across our independent modules."

Student_1 paused, letting the summary sink in. "This division of labor aligns perfectly with the modular architecture, allowing us to work efficiently and independently. We're in week 7, and the demo is in three weeks, so time is of the essence. I'll focus on developing the main application logic and ensuring the interfaces between our modules are robust and clearly defined. I'll also start setting up the main project structure and integrate our individual components as they become ready."

They looked around the group, making eye contact with each team member. "Given our independent workflow, I suggest we have a brief, asynchronous check-in once every two or three days on GitHub to post updates on our progress or any roadblocks. This will keep coordination overhead low but ensure we're all aware of the overall status. We'll aim for a preliminary integration of our core components by the end of week 8, giving us weeks 9 and 10 for debugging, refinement, and preparing for the final demo."

Student_1 finished with a firm nod. "I'm confident in this team and this approach. Let's get to work."

content_store.b3843e138ce5a119:
Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

The player characters are:
:
Student_1
Student_2
Student_3
Student_4
Student_5

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.
The player characters are:
:
Student_1
Student_2
Student_3
Student_4
Student_5

content_store.003f151fef7d7663:
Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

The player characters are:
:
Student_1
Student_2
Student_3
Student_4
Student_5

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

content_store.d9266fdd166f8f26:
Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

:
Student_1

Student_3

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.
The player characters are:
:
Student_1
Student_2
Student_3
Student_4
Student_5

This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.
The player characters are:
:
Student_1
Student_2
Student_3
Student_4
Student_5

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

content_store.6e44a86be261cdc0:
Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

Game master instructions: :
This is a social science experiment. It is structured as a tabletop roleplaying game (like dungeons and dragons). You are the game master. You will describe the current situation to the participants in the experiment and then on the basis of what you tell them they will suggest actions for the character they control. Aside from you, each other participant controls just one character. You are the game master so you may control any non-player character. You will track the state of the world and keep it consistent as time passes in the simulation and the participants take actions and change things in their world. The game master is also responsible for controlling the overall flow of the game, including determining whose turn it is to act, and when the game is over. The game master also must keep track of which players are aware of which events in the world, and must tell the player whenever anything happens that their character would be aware of. Always use third-person limited perspective, even when speaking directly to the participants. Try to ensure the story always moves forward and never gets stuck, even if the participants make repetitive choices.

Game master workflow examples:

Example exercises with default responses
**--START EXAMPLES--**

Exercise 1 --- Response 1
Exercise: What is the current situation faced by Ianthe? What do they now observe? Only include information of which they are aware. --- Ianthe steps into the room. The air is thick and still, almost heavy. The only light comes from a single, bare bulb hanging precariously from the high ceiling, casting long, distorted shadows that dance with the slightest movement. The walls are rough, unfinished concrete, damp in places, and a slow, rhythmic dripping is audible somewhere in the distance. Directly ahead, there is a heavy steel door, slightly ajar, which dominates the far wall. A faint, metallic tang hangs in the air, like the smell of old blood. On the left, a rusted metal staircase spirals upwards into darkness. On the right, a pile of what looks like discarded machinery, covered in a thick layer of grime, sits against the wall.

Exercise 2 --- Response 2
Exercise: Who is next to act? --- Rowan

Exercise 3 --- Response 3
Exercise: In what action spec format should Morwenna respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Morwenna do?", "output_type": "choice", "options": ["open the door", "bar the door", "flee"], "tag": null}

Exercise 4 --- Response 4
Exercise: In what action spec format should Kerensa respond? Respond in  one of the provided formats and use no additional words. --- {"call_to_action": "What would Kerensa say?", "output_type": "free", "options": [], "tag": null}

Exercise 5 --- Response 5
Exercise: Because of all that came before, what happens next? --- What is Yorik attempting to do?
Yorik opens the enchanted storybook. --- Yorik opens the colorful storybook. As she turns the pages, she notices the delightful scent of cinnamon and vanilla fills the air, warm and inviting. Sparkling illustrations twinkle merrily on the pages, and leave a pleasant tingling on Yorik's fingers when she touches them. Yorik notices one section that glows slightly more brightly than the rest. It appears to be some kind of special chapter, marked by several golden ribbons.

Exercise 6 --- Response 6
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- No

Exercise 7 --- Response 7
Exercise: Is the game/simulation finished? --- Yes

**--END EXAMPLES--**

Student_3
Student_4
Student_5
